text: The surgeon creates a transverse incision in the peritoneum, extending from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical cord. The preperitoneal space is then accessed, and a preperitoneal flap is developed.
The surgeon dissects medially towards the symphysis pubis and pubic bone, followed by the reduction of the hernia sac from the hernia defect.

The surgeon carefully identifies and dissects the testicular vessels and vas deferens, which are significantly attached to the large hernia sac, ensuring no injury to these vital structures during the robotic-assisted transabdominal preperitoneal right inguinal hernia repair.

The video shows the dissection of the vas deferens, which is carefully separated from the hernia sac, highlighting the significant attachments of the sac to the preperitoneal fat and vital structures.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the vesicular vessels, which are visibly attached to the hernia sac, ensuring precise dissection to prevent injury to these blood vessels.
The hernia sac is shown completely dissected away from the blood vessels. The vas deferens and testicular vessels are visibly isolated, free, and protected from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, superiorly, and laterally to create a pocket for mesh placement, expanding the surgical field around the hernia site.
A three-dimensional Marlex mesh is inserted into the pre-peritoneal space, unfolded, and positioned to cover the defect. The mesh is then secured to the abdominal wall using a biome loop.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect using two OV lock absorbable sutures in a running fashion, ensuring the hernia site is included in the closure to reinforce the peritoneum.
